about.title: About
about.subtitle: A small helper for the big love you have for your pet.
about.appTagline: Everything about your pet's health in one place.
about.appDescription: Pawly combines a digital health passport with AI food analysis for dogs, cats and other pets. It keeps your vaccinations, medications and vet visits in order — and helps you pick the right food with confidence. So you can focus on what matters most: your pet.
about.privacyDescription: Access is protected by sign-in and all your pet records are private — only you can see them.

support.title: Like Pawly? Support the project
support.body: Pawly is completely free for now. If it helps you take care of your pet, you can support it with a voluntary contribution — it covers our AI and server costs so the app stays ad-free.

aiAnalysis.title: AI food analysis
aiAnalysis.description: Type or snap a photo of the ingredients — in seconds you'll know what's actually in your pet's food.

healthPassport.title: Digital health passport
healthPassport.description: Vaccinations, deworming, medications and vet visits — all clearly in one place.

diary.title: Health diary
diary.description: Record health incidents and track what works and what doesn't.

vetCard.title: Vet card
vetCard.description: A clear summary you can show on your phone or print before the visit.

profiles.title: Pet profiles
profiles.description: Dogs, cats and other pets — each with their own profile and allergy list.

faq.title: FAQ
faq.subtitle: Quick answers about how Pawly works and what happens to your data.

items.a: Your pet profiles and health records are stored in a secure cloud database (Supabase) via our server. They are tied to your account — they are not stored only in the browser.
Only you. Every request is bound to your signed-in account and the database is configured so that no one can access your data without your login.

items.q: Can I access my data from multiple devices?
items.a: Yes. Since the data is in the cloud, after signing in you'll see it on your phone, tablet and computer — always up to date.
Communication uses encrypted HTTPS, access is only possible after sign-in, and database secret keys are exclusively on the server, never in the browser.

items.q: Are scans and photos I upload for recognition stored?
items.a: No. When you upload a photo of a passport or food packaging for AI recognition, it is only used to read the text — you get the result back, but the image itself is not stored on the server.

items.q: What about photos I attach directly to a record or episode?
items.a: Those yes — attachments you deliberately add to a record are saved as part of it, so you can see them later.
Text or photos are processed by AI services (OpenAI and Google Vision), which recognize the content and return the result. We only send what is needed for the evaluation.

items.q: Is the analysis result medical advice?
items.a: No. Results are informational and serve as a guide. For health decisions, always consult a veterinarian.
With email and password, or with a single click via your Google account.

items.q: I forgot my password, what now?
items.a: On the login page click "Forgot password?" and we'll send you an email to set a new one.
Yes. Create a profile for each pet — dogs, cats and other animals, each with their own allergies and records.

items.q: What happens when I delete a pet?
items.a: Along with the profile, all related records are deleted too (vaccinations, medications, episodes, expenses…). This action cannot be undone.

items.q: How do I delete my account and all my data?
items.a: In the side menu click "Delete account". Your account and all saved data will be permanently removed. This action is irreversible.
Yes, the app is fully responsive and you can add it to your home screen from the browser as an app.

hero.chip: For dogs, cats and other pets
hero.description: Vaccinations, dewormings, food and vet visits — all together on your phone. Pawly reminds you what's coming up, and AI tells you whether a food really suits your dog or cat.
hero.dogPhotoAlt: Golden retriever looking at the camera

howItWorks.title: Three steps and the passport's on your phone
howItWorks.subtitle: Simple and free — your first health passport is ready in minutes.

steps.title: Set up a profile
steps.text: Name, breed, age, allergies. Done in a minute.

steps.title: Photograph the old passport
steps.text: AI pulls the vaccinations and dewormings from the photo — no retyping.

steps.title: And you're covered
steps.text: Pawly tracks your due dates and prints a vet card with everything that matters.

features.subtitle: Six things Pawly helps you with — from the bowl to the vet's office.

items.title: Health passport
items.text: Vaccinations, dewormings, ticks and chronic diagnoses, clearly on one timeline.

items.title: AI food analysis
items.text: Snap the packaging and in seconds you know whether the food suits your pet.

items.title: Reminders
items.text: Pawly tells you what's coming up — deworming, vaccinations and vet check-ups.

items.title: Vet card
items.text: Everything that matters on one PDF. Print it or show it right on your phone.

items.title: Can my pet eat…?
items.text: Chocolate? Grapes? Type a food and AI tells you right away if it's safe.

items.title: Health diary
items.text: Note what happened and what worked. Next time, you've got advice from yourself.

comparison.title: Paper passport vs Pawly
comparison.subtitle: Why the worn paper booklet belongs in a drawer.
paperPoints: You have to carry it everywhere
Vet handwriting you often can't read
Tells you nothing about food quality
pawlyPoints: Always on your phone, never lost
Reminds you of vaccinations and dewormings itself
AI reads the passport and checks the food
A vet card in one click
Installs from your browser to phone and desktop

cta.title: Get your pet's health in order today
cta.subtitle: The app is free and ad-free. Add a profile and you can start right away.

appPreview.title: This is what your digital passport looks like
appPreview.subtitle: Works on desktop and mobile — all records synced, always with you.

allPets.subtitle: Dogs, cats, rabbits, birds, reptiles and horses — Pawly handles a health card for every pet.

demo.badge: Try it now — no account needed
demo.title: Ask AI what your pet can eat
demo.subtitle: Type a food or ingredient — instantly see if it's safe for your dog, cat or other pet.
demo.continueBtn: Continue with real AI in the app

fallback.short: Without full AI I can't give a reliable answer.
fallback.explanation: This is just a demo with a limited list of foods. For accurate evaluation, use the real AI in the app.
warnings: Consult your vet before giving any new food

chocolate.short: No, chocolate is toxic to dogs.
chocolate.explanation: It contains theobromine, which is toxic to dogs and can cause vomiting, tremors, seizures or heart failure.

grapes.short: No, grapes can cause kidney failure.
grapes.explanation: Even a small amount of grapes or raisins can cause acute kidney failure in dogs.

onion.short: No, onions damage red blood cells.
onion.explanation: Onions, garlic and chives contain thiosulfates that break down red blood cells and can cause anaemia.

xylitol.explanation: The sweetener xylitol rapidly lowers blood sugar and can cause liver failure even in small doses.

carrot.short: Yes, carrots are an excellent snack.
carrot.explanation: Carrots are low in calories, contain fibre and beta-carotene. Raw carrots also help clean teeth.

apple.short: Yes, but remove the seeds and stem.
apple.explanation: Apples are healthy and tasty, but the seeds contain traces of cyanide. Only give the fleshy pieces.

banana.explanation: Rich in potassium and vitamins. Give only in moderate amounts due to high sugar content.

chicken bones.short: Depends on preparation — never give cooked.
chicken bones.explanation: Cooked bones splinter and can injure the digestive tract. Raw large bones are acceptable under supervision.
warnings: Cooked bones can injure the digestive tract
Only raw and larger bones under supervision

milk.explanation: Some dogs digest milk fine, others get diarrhoea. Try a small amount and monitor the reaction.

cheese.explanation: Hard cheeses low in lactose are OK as treats. Watch out for salt content.
warnings: Not for dogs with dairy allergies

avocado.explanation: Persin in avocado can cause digestive problems and vomiting. The pit is also a choking hazard.

salmon.short: Yes, but only cooked and boneless.
salmon.explanation: Salmon is an excellent source of omega-3 fatty acids. Raw salmon may contain parasites.

peanuts.explanation: Roasted unsalted shelled peanuts are fine in small amounts. With peanut butter, check that it does not contain the sweetener xylitol.

egg.short: Yes, a cooked egg is a great protein source.
egg.explanation: A cooked egg is safe and nutritious. Feed raw with caution due to the salmonella risk.

tomato.short: Ripe flesh yes, green parts no.
tomato.explanation: Ripe tomato in small amounts is fine. The leaves and unripe green fruit contain solanine, which is toxic.

potato.explanation: Cooked, unsalted potato is fine. Raw and green potatoes contain solanine.

garlic.short: No, garlic damages red blood cells.
garlic.explanation: Garlic and onion contain compounds that break down red blood cells and can cause anemia.

tuna.explanation: A small amount of cooked tuna is okay, but it contains mercury. Don't feed often, and not salted or canned in oil.

beef.short: Yes, lean cooked beef is excellent.
beef.explanation: A quality protein source. Serve without seasoning or excess fat.

rice.short: Yes, cooked rice is easy to digest.
rice.explanation: Plain cooked white rice helps with digestive upset. Serve without salt or seasoning.

bread.short: A small piece now and then is fine.
bread.explanation: Plain wheat bread in small amounts is harmless but has no nutritional value. Never feed raw dough.

cucumber.short: Yes, cucumber is a low-calorie snack.
cucumber.explanation: Cucumber is healthy and hydrating, ideal as a light treat.

broccoli.explanation: Broccoli is healthy, but in larger amounts it irritates the stomach. Serve only as a small addition.

pumpkin.explanation: Pumpkin is rich in fiber and helps with both diarrhea and constipation.

watermelon.short: Yes, but no rind or seeds.
watermelon.explanation: Watermelon flesh is refreshing and safe. Remove the rind and seeds.

strawberries.short: Yes, strawberries are fine in moderation.
strawberries.explanation: They contain vitamins and fiber. Due to the sugar, feed only in small amounts.

blueberries.short: Yes, blueberries are a great antioxidant.
blueberries.explanation: Low in calories and full of antioxidants — a great healthy treat.

orange.short: A small piece of flesh, yes.
orange.explanation: Peeled, seedless flesh is okay in small amounts, but acidic fruit doesn't suit every pet.

pear.explanation: Pear is healthy and juicy. Remove the seeds, as they contain traces of cyanide.

nuts.explanation: Macadamia and walnuts can be toxic, and the high fat content risks pancreatitis.

almonds.explanation: Pets don't digest almonds well; they risk stomach upset and choking. Best avoided.

honey.explanation: Honey is safe in small amounts, but it's pure sugar. Not for puppies or diabetics.

pineapple.explanation: Fresh flesh is a source of vitamins. Due to the sugar, only in small amounts.

coffee.short: No, caffeine is toxic to pets.
coffee.explanation: Coffee, tea and energy drinks contain caffeine, which causes a racing heart, tremors and seizures.

alcohol.short: Never — even a small amount is dangerous.
alcohol.explanation: Alcohol causes poisoning, vomiting and respiratory depression even at a small dose.

dough.short: No, raw yeast dough is dangerous.
dough.explanation: Yeast ferments in the stomach, producing alcohol and gas — risking bloat and poisoning.

raisins.short: No, raisins can cause kidney failure.
raisins.explanation: Like grapes, raisins can cause acute kidney failure.

ham.explanation: Ham and cured meats are high in salt and fat, straining the kidneys and pancreas.

ice cream.short: Better not, due to sugar and lactose.
ice cream.explanation: The milk and sugar in ice cream don't suit many pets. If anything, a sugar-free dog popsicle.

yogurt.short: Plain unsweetened, in small amounts, yes.
yogurt.explanation: Plain unsweetened yogurt with probiotics is okay in small amounts if the pet tolerates lactose.

mushrooms.explanation: Cultivated edible mushrooms are harmless, but wild ones can be deadly poisonous.

corn.short: Yes, but not on the cob.
corn.explanation: Cooked corn kernels are harmless. Never feed the whole cob — it risks an intestinal blockage.

peas.short: Yes, peas are a healthy addition.
peas.explanation: Fresh or frozen peas contain vitamins and fiber.

spinach.explanation: Spinach contains oxalates, which strain the kidneys in larger amounts. Small amounts only.

peach.explanation: Ripe flesh is okay, but the pit contains cyanide and is a choking hazard.

productDemo.title: See what the AI pulls from a food label
productDemo.subtitle: Pick a sample food and see exactly what you get inside the app — a quality score, an ingredient breakdown, and an allergen warning based on your pet's profile.
productDemo.ctaNote: Free — just create a pet profile.

samples.name: Salmon & sweet potato
samples.summary: A quality grain-free food with a clear protein source listed first. Suitable even for dogs with sensitive digestion.
pros: A named meat source listed first

samples.name: Chicken kibble
samples.summary: Grains and unnamed meat meals dominate. A low share of quality animal protein.

allergen.message: Contains chicken — Nalina has a chicken allergy recorded in the profile.

samples.name: Beef & rice
samples.summary: A decent price-to-quality ratio. Contains grains, but with a named meat source and added probiotics.

passportScan.title: Photograph the old passport, AI does the rest
passportScan.subtitle: From a photo of the vaccination passport, AI pulls out vaccinations, dewormings and dates — no manual retyping.
passportScan.idleHint: Click and AI reads the passport for you.

vetCard.title: Everything essential on one card
vetCard.subtitle: Show it on your phone or print it before the visit — the vet has the full picture instantly.
vetCard.ctaNote: Free — ready to print or show on mobile.

reminders.title: Never miss a vaccination or deworming
reminders.subtitle: Pawly keeps track of the dates for you and reminds you in time what's coming up — for each pet separately.
reminders.footerNote: We'll remind you by email in advance, based on your settings.
reminders.ctaNote: Free — your first passport in minutes.

notifications.title: Notifications
notifications.description: We'll remind you when vaccinations, deworming, antiparasitic treatments, vet check-ups, or medication end dates are approaching.
notifications.emailNoEmail: Your account has no email — add one by signing in with email.
notifications.leadDaysNote: Pick a preset or add your own number of days (1–365). We'll also remind you on the due date and the day after.
notifications.invalidLeadDay: Enter a number of days from 1 to 365.
notifications.minLeadDaysError: Keep at least one reminder interval.
notifications.pushComing: Push notifications to your phone are coming soon.
